relationship_summary: The Russia-Iran relationship is best understood as a transactional convergence of interests rather than a military alliance. Russia's support for Iran is instrumentalized around a structural logic: an Iran that destabilizes US strategic attention, consumes Western air defense inventories, and disrupts oil markets through the Strait of Hormuz directly benefits Russia regardless of Iran's military fortunes. Higher oil prices translate into billions in additional Russian state revenue — Urals crude rose from ~$40/barrel to above $70/barrel after the February 2026 conflict began, generating approximately $150 million per day in windfall revenue. Western attention and munitions diverted to the Middle East reduce support available for Ukraine. Russia therefore has a strong incentive to sustain Iran as a functional adversary of the United States without committing to Iran's survival as an existential obligation. The relationship deepened significantly after Russia's 2022 invasion of Ukraine created mutual dependencies — Iran needed advanced platforms Russia could supply, and Russia needed cheap drone technology Iran had perfected. But this is not NATO-style collective defense. There is no mutual defense clause, no forward-deployed Russian forces in Iran, and no Russian commitment to intervene if Iran faces regime-threatening defeat. The January 2025 Comprehensive Strategic Partnership Treaty formalized cooperation across defense, energy, and economic sectors, but its operational significance remains untested. Both sides understand the relationship's limits: Russia will help Iran enough to keep it fighting, but not enough to risk direct confrontation with the United States.

russian_support_provided.description: Russia's confirmed military transfers to Iran as of January 2026 fall into several categories. Air defense: Russia completed delivery of the S-300PMU2 system in October 2016 under an $800 million contract originally signed in 2007 (delayed 2010–2015 due to UN sanctions and Russian compliance). Iran deployed these batteries to defend nuclear facilities including Fordow. Israel disabled all of Iran's S-300 batteries during the October 2024 strikes. Post-2024, Russia has delivered or is delivering S-400 components, though the status of full system integration is uncertain. Fighter aircraft: Leaked Russian defense correspondence (October–November 2025, from the Rostec/Black Mirror leak) confirmed a contract for 48 Su-35 fighter jets valued at approximately €6 billion. Production of the first 16 aircraft began at the Komsomolsk-on-Amur plant in 2024, with prepayments completed across three tranches in 2024 and deliveries scheduled 2025–2027. The first two Su-35SE airframes were reportedly handed over in November 2024 for transport to Iran. At least one squadron of Yak-130 training jets has been delivered, and up to 6 Mi-28 attack helicopters arrived by January 2026. A €500 million deal for 500 Verba MANPADS was signed in December 2025. Russia has also provided the Rezonans-NE over-the-horizon radar system. Iran's dependency on Russian military hardware is acute for advanced air defense and combat aviation — these are capabilities Iran cannot produce domestically at comparable quality — but limited for drones and ballistic missiles, which Iran manufactures indigenously.
russian_support_provided.source: S-300 delivery: Arms Control Association (Nov 2016), CSIS Missile Threat. S-400 components: multiple US intelligence sources via Washington Post, CNN (March 2026). Su-35 deal: leaked Rostec documents (Black Mirror hack, October 2025); United24 investigation (November 28, 2025); IRGC aerospace commander public confirmation (January 2025). Yak-130/Mi-28: Breaking Defense (November 2023), Army Recognition (October 2024). Verba MANPADS: project knowledge verified pre-war assessment. Rezonans-NE: CNN, March 6 2026 multi-source reporting.

russian_support_provided.description: Drone technology represents the most unusual dimension of the relationship — a case of reverse technology flow followed by Russian improvement and partial return transfer. Iran supplied Russia with Shahed-136 and Mohajer-6 drones beginning in mid-2022. Russia paid approximately $1.75 billion for 6,000 drones and production line technology, with payments including gold bullion (documented in leaked contracts showing 1.78 tons + 2 tons of gold in February payments, plus an initial €140 million cash transfer and captured Western weapons). Russia established domestic production at the Alabuga Special Economic Zone in Tatarstan, initially with Iranian blueprints, source code, and training personnel under a November 2022 agreement with the MODAFL front company Sahara Thunder. By mid-2023, Russia was producing its own Geran-2 variants and had begun modifying the design with better navigation, anti-jamming systems, jet engines (Shahed-238 variant), AI computing platforms, decoy variants (Parodya and Gerbera), and even Starlink devices found in debris. Over 38,000 Shahed-type drones were launched by Russia in 2025 alone. As of March 2026, Russia is confirmed to be sending improved Shahed variants back to Iran — drones that originated as Iranian technology but were refined through years of Russian combat testing in Ukraine. This represents a unique bilateral technology feedback loop.
russian_support_provided.source: Initial Iran-to-Russia transfers: US NSC (Jake Sullivan, July 2022), DIA assessment (July 2023, 400+ drones confirmed). Gold payments: Haaretz leaked documents investigation (February 2024). Alabuga factory: US intelligence/John Kirby (May 2023), ISIS production analysis. 38,000 figure: Ukrainian data cited by Wikipedia/Shahed drones entry. Russia-to-Iran reverse transfer: AP (March 2026, US and European officials), confirmed 'very active' discussions on drone shipments. Modifications: Conflict Armament Research, Ukrainian military analysis of debris.

russian_support_provided.description: Russia has provided Iran with significant diplomatic cover, though this support is calibrated rather than unconditional. Russia has consistently used its UN Security Council veto to block or water down resolutions targeting Iran's nuclear program and military activities. The January 2025 Comprehensive Strategic Partnership Treaty — signed by Putin and Pezeshkian — formalized bilateral cooperation across defense, energy, and economic domains. However, Russia notably did not block UNSC Resolution 1929 in 2010 (which it used as a pretext to cancel the S-300 delivery), demonstrating that Moscow will sacrifice Iranian interests when its relationship with the West requires it. Russia's diplomatic support during the 2026 conflict has been limited to intelligence sharing and arms transfers rather than overt diplomatic intervention or mediation efforts. Russia has not proposed ceasefire resolutions or inserted itself as a diplomatic broker, suggesting it prefers the conflict to continue generating strategic benefits rather than reaching resolution.
russian_support_provided.source: Strategic Partnership Treaty: Iran International, Army Recognition (October 2024 BRICS summit context; January 2025 signing). UNSC voting record: Arms Control Association. Diplomatic posture during 2026 conflict: assessed from absence of Russian mediation initiatives in verified reporting.

russian_support_provided.description: Russia is actively providing real-time intelligence to Iran to target US forces during the 2026 conflict. This was independently confirmed on March 6, 2026, by the Washington Post (three US officials), CNN (multiple sources), NBC News (four sources), and AP (two officials). Russia is sharing satellite imagery and targeting information including locations and movements of American troops, ships, and aircraft. The Kanopus-V satellite — originally launched as a joint project and re-designated 'Khayyam' for Iranian operations — provides round-the-clock optical and radar imagery. By March 17, the Wall Street Journal reported Russia had expanded intelligence sharing to include improved drone guidance data enabling more precise Iranian strikes on radar and missile systems in the Gulf. This intelligence support mirrors what the US provides Ukraine regarding Russian force positions, making it operationally significant. The Khayyam spy satellite, launched in August 2022 aboard a Russian Soyuz rocket from Baikonur, was initially presented as a civilian Earth observation satellite, but Western intelligence agencies assessed from the outset that Russia retained access to its imagery feeds. Iran's dependency on Russian intelligence is high for maritime and air domain awareness against US forces, as Iran's own satellite reconnaissance capabilities are limited.
russian_support_provided.source: Intelligence sharing confirmation: Washington Post, CNN, NBC News, AP (all March 6, 2026, multiple independent US intelligence sources). Expansion to drone guidance: Wall Street Journal (March 17, 2026). Khayyam satellite: launched August 2022, Western intelligence assessments of dual-use nature widely reported.

russian_support_provided.description: Russia's economic support for Iran is indirect but significant. The most consequential development is the US sanctions waiver on Russian oil issued March 13, 2026 (Treasury General License GL 134), which Russia obtained as a direct consequence of the Iran conflict disrupting global oil markets. This waiver covers approximately 120–128 million barrels of Russian oil on water, temporarily pauses the US price cap, and was issued specifically to 'stabilize global energy markets' disrupted by the Strait of Hormuz closure. Russia's oil revenue windfall from the conflict is estimated at $150 million per day, with potential annual additional revenue of ~$55 billion if prices average $90/barrel. Indian imports of Russian crude reached ~1.5 million barrels per day by mid-March 2026, up ~50% from early February. Russia and Iran also maintain bilateral trade arrangements including barter deals (Iranian oil for Russian goods, discussed since 2015). The January 2025 treaty includes economic cooperation provisions. However, Russia has not provided Iran with direct financial assistance or sanctions-busting mechanisms comparable to what China provides through continued oil purchases via shadow fleets.
russian_support_provided.source: Sanctions waiver: Treasury GL 134 (March 13, 2026). Revenue estimates: Carnegie Russia Eurasia Center analyst Sergey Vakulenko. Indian import data: Bloomberg, mid-March 2026. Barter arrangements: Jamestown Foundation (2015), Crescent International.

hard_limits.description: Russia will not undertake several categories of action even if Iran faces existential military defeat. First, direct military intervention: Russia will not deploy combat forces to fight alongside Iran against the United States or Israel. There are no Russian troops in Iran, no Russian air defense crews operating Iranian systems, and no Russian naval forces engaged in Strait of Hormuz defense. This contrasts with Russia's direct military role in Syria (2015–present), where Russian forces operated air defense systems, flew combat missions, and maintained ground personnel. The difference is revealing — Syria represented a low-cost intervention against non-state actors and a weak state military, while Iran would mean direct confrontation with the US military. Second, nuclear umbrella extension: Russia has not and will not extend nuclear deterrence to cover Iran. Russia's nuclear doctrine covers Russian territory and allies in formal collective security arrangements (CSTO members); Iran is in neither category. There is no equivalent of NATO's Article 5 in any Russia-Iran agreement. Third, forward deployment: Russia will not station forces in Iran for deterrent purposes. This would be escalatory beyond anything Moscow has signaled willingness to accept. Russia's stated position emphasizes 'multipolarity' and opposition to US hegemony, but its actual red line is avoiding scenarios that could lead to direct Russia-US military confrontation outside of the Ukraine theater. Russia's calculus is that Iran's value is maximized as an independent actor absorbing US resources — deploying Russian forces would transform a proxy benefit into a direct liability.
hard_limits.ukraine_constraint: Russia's commitment in Ukraine severely constrains its ability to support Iran even if political will existed. Russia's defense industrial base is operating at wartime capacity to sustain operations in Ukraine, producing weapons in the same categories Iran needs most: air defense systems, ballistic missiles, and drones. Russia launched over 38,000 Shahed-type drones against Ukraine in 2025 alone, consuming enormous production capacity. Russia's own air force has lost over a dozen Su-35s in Ukraine, creating domestic replacement demand that competes directly with the Iranian export order. The Komsomolsk-on-Amur plant historically produces roughly 14 aircraft per year and is attempting to expand, but simultaneously filling Russian military orders, Iranian contracts, and other export commitments (Algeria, potentially Ethiopia). Russia's overall arms exports dropped 92% between 2021 and 2024 according to the Jamestown Foundation, leaving only about a dozen active buyers — a reflection of production being consumed domestically. Large-scale wartime arms transfers to Iran have not been confirmed precisely because both countries consume the same weapons categories and Russia's industrial capacity is stretched. The attention constraint is equally significant: Russia's senior military and political leadership is consumed by the Ukraine campaign, with limited bandwidth for managing a second major theater. Elite Russian airborne units were being redeployed from offensive sectors to counter Ukrainian counterattacks during the same period as the Iran conflict, suggesting manpower exhaustion rather than strategic flexibility.

irans_perception: Tehran's leadership views Russian support as useful but fundamentally unreliable, a perception grounded in decades of experience. The S-300 saga is the foundational grievance: Russia signed the contract in 2007, cancelled it in 2010 under Western pressure (citing UNSC Resolution 1929 that Russia itself voted for), endured a $4 billion Iranian lawsuit, and only delivered the system in 2016 after the JCPOA made it politically convenient. This nine-year delay — during which Iran was most vulnerable to Israeli/US strikes on its nuclear program — demonstrated that Moscow would sacrifice Iranian security interests when its Western relationships required it. Iran responded by developing the Bavar-373 as a domestic alternative, reflecting a broader strategic lesson: never depend on Russia for time-critical capabilities. The drone technology transfer reversed the dependency dynamic temporarily — Iran leveraged its indispensable role as a Shahed supplier to extract the Su-35 deal, Mi-28 helicopters, Yak-130 trainers, and other concessions from a sanctions-weakened Russia desperate for partners. Iran effectively traded drone technology and production know-how worth approximately $1.75 billion for commitments worth over $6.5 billion in advanced aircraft alone. However, reports from Iran International (October 2025) indicate that Khamenei sent a 'message of reproach' to Putin via Foreign Minister Araghchi regarding Russia's performance during the June 2025 twelve-day war, when Russian-supplied S-300 batteries were destroyed by Israel with apparent ease and Russia provided no material intervention. Iranian lawmakers have publicly expressed frustration, with one noting Russia 'has a particular inclination toward Israel' and questioning why Moscow withheld advanced systems. The IRGC likely assesses that Russian intelligence sharing during the 2026 conflict is valuable but motivated by Russia's own interest in US overextension rather than genuine concern for Iranian outcomes.
escalation_thresholds: Russia's support would increase if: (1) the US-Iran conflict significantly escalates in ways that further divert Western resources from Ukraine — Russia would expand intelligence sharing, accelerate weapons deliveries already in the pipeline, and potentially provide more advanced electronic warfare capabilities; (2) the US imposes costs on Russia for its intelligence support without offering concessions — Russia would have no incentive to moderate and might escalate to include more direct targeting assistance; (3) oil prices spike further — every $10/month increase generates approximately $1.63 billion in additional Russian state revenue, creating a direct financial incentive to prolong the conflict. Russia would decrease or quietly disengage support if: (1) Iran suffers a decisive military defeat that makes continued investment pointless — Russia would redirect resources rather than double down on a losing position; (2) the US offers a credible Ukraine deal conditioned on Russia withdrawing Iran support — the Ukraine conflict is Moscow's primary strategic concern and would take precedence; (3) Iran's conflict begins to threaten Russian equities directly, such as if fighting spreads to the Caspian region or if Russian-flagged vessels are targeted in the Gulf; (4) China signals that the conflict threatens Chinese interests (energy flows through Hormuz) and pressures Russia to help de-escalate — Russia values the China relationship more than the Iran relationship. The key US action that would cause Russian escalation is attempting to punish Russia directly for intelligence sharing (strikes on Russian satellite infrastructure, severe new sanctions) without offering an off-ramp — this would harden Moscow's commitment. The key US action that would cause Russian disengagement is a diplomatic package linking Ukraine settlement terms to Russian withdrawal of Iran support, giving Moscow something it wants more than the Iran conflict provides.

documented_instances.what_happened: Iran and Russia signed an $800 million contract for five S-300PMU-1 battalions (approximately 40–60 transporter-erector-launchers), intended to defend Iranian nuclear facilities and strategic sites.
documented_instances.significance: Marked the first major Russian commitment to enhance Iran's air defense capabilities. The system would have significantly complicated any US/Israeli strike planning against Iranian nuclear sites.
documented_instances.source: Arms Control Association, Washington Institute for Near East Policy, CSIS Missile Threat

documented_instances.event: Russia cancels S-300 delivery under Western pressure
documented_instances.what_happened: President Medvedev suspended the S-300 agreement, incorporating UNSC Resolution 1929 (which Russia voted for) into Russian law as justification. Iran filed a $4 billion lawsuit against Rosoboronexport in Geneva.
documented_instances.significance: The foundational betrayal in Iranian perception of Russian reliability. Demonstrated that Moscow would prioritize its Western relationships over Iranian security commitments. Iran accelerated development of the domestic Bavar-373 system as a hedge against Russian unreliability.
documented_instances.source: Jamestown Foundation, Arms Control Association, Crescent International

documented_instances.event: Putin lifts S-300 ban, deliveries begin
documented_instances.what_happened: Following the JCPOA nuclear agreement, Putin signed a decree lifting the self-imposed ban on S-300 deliveries. Components began arriving via the Caspian port of Anzali in April 2016. Four S-300PMU2 batteries were fully delivered by October 2016, each with target-acquisition radar, engagement radar, and four towed TELs. Iran deployed batteries around the Fordow nuclear enrichment facility.
documented_instances.significance: Delivery came only after it was politically cost-free for Russia (post-JCPOA, with US acquiescence). Confirmed Iranian assessment that Russia acts on its own timeline and interests, not Iran's security needs.
documented_instances.source: Washington Post (May 2016), The Diplomat (July 2017), Washington Institute (April 2016), CSIS

documented_instances.event: Iran begins supplying Shahed drones to Russia for use in Ukraine
documented_instances.what_happened: Following Russian delegation visits to Kashan Airfield in June–July 2022, Iran transferred Shahed-136, Shahed-131, and Mohajer-6 drones to Russia. First combat use documented September 2022 in Chuhuiv, Ukraine. Initial transfer included approximately 160 drones for $145 million plus captured Western weapons. Iran publicly denied transfers while Foreign Minister Abdollahian admitted in November 2022 that Iran shipped 'a small number' of drones before the war.
documented_instances.significance: Reversed the traditional patron-client dynamic — Iran became an indispensable supplier to Russia. This leverage enabled Iran to extract major concessions including the Su-35 deal. Also deepened Iran's international isolation and led to Ukrainian diplomatic downgrade.
documented_instances.source: US NSC (Jake Sullivan, July 2022 initial warning), DIA assessment (July 2023), Conflict Armament Research, Sky News (€140M cash report), AP

documented_instances.event: Alabuga drone factory agreement and production localization
documented_instances.what_happened: MODAFL front company Sahara Thunder concluded a major agreement with Russia's Alabuga Special Economic Zone in Tatarstan for domestic Shahed production. Iran transferred blueprints, source code, production equipment, and extensive training. The deal covered 6,000 drones at $193,000 each ($1.75 billion total, paid partly in gold bullion). The Yelabuga factory aimed to produce 310 drones/month at full capacity. By August 2023, Russia was deploying domestically-produced Geran-2 variants.
documented_instances.significance: Shifted Iran-Russia drone cooperation from episodic supply to sustained industrial partnership. Gave Russia independent production capability, reducing dependency on Iranian imports while maintaining the technology relationship. Created a feedback loop where Russian modifications would later benefit Iran.
documented_instances.source: Haaretz leaked documents (February 2024, gold payment receipts), US intelligence/John Kirby (May 2023), Conflict Armament Research (August 2023), Just Security legal analysis (March 2026)

documented_instances.what_happened: Russia launched the Khayyam satellite (based on the Kanopus-V platform) from Baikonur Cosmodrome aboard a Soyuz rocket, officially presented as an Iranian civilian Earth observation satellite. Western intelligence agencies assessed from launch that Russia retained access to the imagery feeds, effectively creating a dual-use reconnaissance asset.
documented_instances.significance: Provided the foundation for the intelligence-sharing relationship that became operationally critical during the 2026 conflict. The satellite provides round-the-clock optical and radar imagery used to track US force movements. Demonstrates how Russia establishes intelligence dependencies that give it ongoing leverage.
documented_instances.source: Launch widely reported August 2022. Intelligence assessment of dual use: multiple Western intelligence agency assessments. Operational use in 2026: Washington Post, CNN, NBC, AP (March 6, 2026)

documented_instances.event: Israel destroys all Iranian S-300 batteries
documented_instances.what_happened: Israeli aircraft disabled all of Iran's Russian-supplied S-300 anti-aircraft missile systems during a multi-wave airstrike. Israeli cyber operations reportedly breached Iranian radar systems, causing screens to freeze before the strikes hit. Iran's most expensive Russian-supplied defensive systems were neutralized in a single operation.
documented_instances.significance: Catastrophic for Iranian confidence in Russian military hardware. Demonstrated that the S-300 — which took nine years to deliver — could be defeated by Israeli electronic warfare and precision strikes. Accelerated Iranian demands for S-400 and Su-35 deliveries. Reports indicate Khamenei sent a 'message of reproach' to Putin regarding Russia's failure to provide adequate systems or support during the June 2025 war.
documented_instances.source: Wikipedia/S-300 entry citing Israeli military reporting. Iran International (October 2025, Araghchi message of reproach). Project knowledge verified assessment.

documented_instances.event: Russia-Iran Comprehensive Strategic Partnership Treaty signed
documented_instances.what_happened: Putin and Iranian President Pezeshkian signed a comprehensive strategic partnership treaty covering defense, energy, and economic cooperation. The treaty formalized the relationship that had deepened since 2022 but remained informal and contract-based.
documented_instances.significance: The treaty represents the highest level of formalization in Russia-Iran relations, but its practical significance is untested. It does not include mutual defense obligations comparable to NATO Article 5 or CSTO commitments. Its value is primarily political signaling of alignment rather than binding operational commitment.
documented_instances.source: Newsweek, Iran International, Army Recognition (October 2024 BRICS context, January 2025 signing)

documented_instances.what_happened: Russia and Iran concluded a €500 million contract for 500 Verba man-portable air defense systems, one of the largest MANPADS deals in recent history.
documented_instances.significance: Indicates continued deepening of arms transfers in the period immediately preceding the February 2026 conflict. Verba systems would be useful against low-flying aircraft and helicopters in a conflict scenario. Timing suggests both sides were preparing for potential escalation.
documented_instances.source: Project knowledge verified assessment (pre-war confirmed arms transfers)

documented_instances.event: Russia confirmed providing real-time intelligence to Iran targeting US forces
documented_instances.what_happened: Multiple independent US intelligence sources confirmed Russia was sharing satellite imagery and targeting information with Iran including locations and movements of US troops, ships, and aircraft. The Khayyam/Kanopus-V satellite provided the primary feed. By March 17, Wall Street Journal reported Russia expanded sharing to include improved drone guidance data enabling more precise strikes on US radar and missile systems in the Gulf.
documented_instances.significance: Represents the most direct Russian support to an adversary actively fighting US forces since the Cold War. Crosses a threshold from arms sales and diplomatic support into active operational collaboration against US military personnel. However, it remains deniable and below the level that would trigger direct US retaliation against Russia — demonstrating Moscow's careful calibration of escalation.
documented_instances.source: Washington Post (3 US officials), CNN (multiple sources), NBC News (4 sources), AP (2 officials) — all March 6, 2026. Wall Street Journal (March 17, 2026)

documented_instances.event: US issues 30-day sanctions waiver on Russian oil
documented_instances.what_happened: The Trump administration issued Treasury General License GL 134, authorizing delivery and sale of Russian crude oil and petroleum products loaded on vessels as of March 12, covering approximately 120–128 million barrels across ~30 global locations. The US price cap on Russian oil was temporarily paused. Treasury Secretary Bessent described it as 'narrowly tailored' to stabilize energy markets disrupted by the Strait of Hormuz closure.
documented_instances.significance: Arguably the single greatest strategic windfall Russia has gained from the Iran conflict. The US effectively rewarded Russia for the very crisis Russia was helping to sustain through intelligence sharing with Iran. European allies reacted with sharp criticism. Russia's oil revenue roughly doubled from pre-conflict levels. Demonstrates the structural dynamic: Russia benefits from Iran conflict regardless of outcome, and the US response to the crisis can inadvertently strengthen Russia's position.
documented_instances.source: Treasury GL 134 (March 13, 2026). European reactions: António Costa, Friedrich Merz, Macron statements. Zelensky warning. Revenue analysis: Carnegie Russia Eurasia Center (Sergey Vakulenko)

documented_instances.event: Russia sends improved Shahed drones back to Iran
documented_instances.what_happened: US and European officials confirmed to AP that Russia was sending upgraded Shahed drone variants to Iran — drones originally based on Iranian designs but improved through years of Russian combat use in Ukraine. Improvements include better navigation, anti-jamming systems, and refined flight profiles. Russian and Iranian officials had 'very active' discussions regarding transfers.
documented_instances.significance: Completes the drone technology feedback loop: Iran gave Russia Shahed technology in 2022, Russia refined it through combat testing against Ukrainian defenses, and is now returning improved versions for use against US forces. Illustrates the practical depth of military-technical cooperation, even as large-scale wartime arms transfers remain limited by Russian industrial constraints.
documented_instances.source: AP (March 2026, US and European officials on condition of anonymity). Project knowledge verified assessment.
